goal_06.config.title: Water quality: A foundation for health and development
goal_06.config.abstract: Access to safe drinking water is essential for health and development, yet over two billion people, mostly in low- and middle-income countries, still lack it. Many remain exposed to contaminated water, which threatens health, livelihoods, and economic progress, underscoring the urgent need to ensure not only access, but also water safety.
goal_06.config.citation: Kashiwase, Haruna and Tony Fujs. 2026. “Water quality: A foundation for health and development”.  In Atlas of Global Development 2026, edited by A. F. Pirlea, U. Serajuddin, A. Thudt, D. Wadhwa, and M. Welch. Washington, DC: World Bank. License: Creative Commons Attribution CC BY 3.0 IGO.
goal_06.config.inputs: Christian Borja-Vega, Claire Chase, Pavel Luengas Sierra and Fan Zhang
goal_06.config.homeFactoidText: countries have water quality as the main constraint in attaining universal access to safely managed drinking water
goal_06.config.homeTagline: Water quality remains the main barrier to safely managed drinking water
goal_06.content.#0.text: Poor water quality is a global concern
goal_06.content.#1.text: Reliable access to safely managed drinking water (clean, safe  and available at home) is essential for health and well-being. While such access is often taken for granted in high-income countries, 2.1 billion people, about a quarter of the world’s population, still rely on water sources that may be far away, irregular, or contaminated.
goal_06.content.#2.title: The number of people without access to safely managed drinking water in the world is
goal_06.content.#3.text: Poor water quality carries significant human and economic costs. Contaminated drinking water is a major driver of preventable disease. It poses serious health risks, especially for infants and young children, with potential long-term consequences for health and cognitive development [reference: chemical_risks]. These risks are global and affect all regions, including high-income countries. [reference: ecoli_wy].
goal_06.content.#4.text: What is considered safe drinking water?
goal_06.content.#5.text: Evaluating the quality of drinking water involves looking at both [emphasis: water sources] meaning the type of infrastructure providing water, and [emphasis: water services ]meaning how well this infrastructure performs for households.
goal_06.content.#6.text: Water sources are classified as improved or unimproved. [emphasis: Improved sources] are those which are designed to reduce the risk of contamination, like pipes, tube wells, or protected wells or springs. [emphasis: Unimproved sources] include unprotected wells and springs or surface water (rivers, lakes, and ponds). A water source which is classified as "improved" is more likely to be protected from outside contamination; however, this does not ensure safe water quality and does not consider accessibility or availability.  For water to be considered safe it needs to meet additional requirements of availability, location, and water quality, which are measured through a [emphasis: water service classification].
goal_06.content.#7.text: Drinking water services are classified into five levels[reference: water_levels]: [emphasis: Safely managed], [emphasis: Basic], [emphasis: Limited], [emphasis: Unimproved], and [emphasis: Surface water].
goal_06.content.#8.title: How access to drinking water is classified into five service levels
goal_06.content.#8.subtitle: Global drinking water access by service level (% of global population)
goal_06.content.#8.source: WHO/UNICEF JMP for Water Supply, Sanitation and Hygiene. Retrieved from World Development Indicators (SH.H2O.SMDW.ZS).
goal_06.content.#8.keyFactLabel: of the world’s population had access to safely managed drinking water in 2024.
goal_06.content.#8.yAxisTitle: Global drinking water access by service level
goal_06.content.#8.scenes.#0.text: [emphasis: Safely managed] is the highest service level: water from an improved source, [emphasis: available on premises],[emphasis: available when needed, ]and [emphasis: free from fecal and priority chemical][footnote: Priority chemicals are substances —arsenic and fluoride—defined by WHO guidelines that may occur naturally in groundwater or enter water sources through pollution and can harm human health if present at high concentrations.][emphasis: contamination]. Global access rose from 60 percent (3.8 billion people) in 2000 to 75 percent (6 billion) in 2024.
goal_06.content.#8.scenes.#1.text: In 2024, about 18 percent of the world’s population (1.4 billion people) used[emphasis: ][emphasis: basic][emphasis: ]drinking water. This is water from and improved sources, often off-premises but within a 30-minute round trip and without guaranteed water quality.
goal_06.content.#8.scenes.#2.text: In 2024, about 9 percent of the world’s population (nearly 700 million people) lacked basic drinking water: around 4% used [emphasis: limited][emphasis: ] services (an improved source, but with a collection time over 30 minutes), 4 percent relied on [emphasis: unimproved ] sources (unprotected well or spring), and about 1 percent depended on [emphasis: surface] water (directly from river, dam, lake), which poses particularly high contamination risks.
goal_06.content.#9.text: Safely managed drinking water is defined as the proportion of the population using an improved water source, that is: accessible on premises[footnote:  This refers to a water source where the point of collection is located within the dwelling, compound, yard, or plot, or where water is delivered directly to the household.],available when needed, and free from fecal and priority chemical contamination.
goal_06.content.#10.text: Although global access to safely managed drinking water has improved, significant disparities remain. Rural dwellers remain behind urban dwellers in access to safely managed drinking water.
goal_06.content.#11.title: Access to safely managed drinking water remains much lower in rural areas
goal_06.content.#11.subtitle: People using safely managed drinking water services by residence (% of population)
goal_06.content.#11.source: WHO/UNICEF JMP for Water Supply, Sanitation and Hygiene. Retrieved from World Development Indicators (SH.H2O.SMDW.RU.ZS, SH.H2O.SMDW.UR.ZS).
goal_06.content.#11.note: Rural population data are unavailable for North America (2000–24) and East Asia & Pacific (2000–14).
goal_06.content.#11.scenes.#0.text: While the proportion of urban dwellers with safely managed drinking water has stagnated at around 83 percent between 2000 and 2024, the share of rural dwellers increased substantially, from 42 percent to 60 percent over the same period. Even then, rural access lags behind urban access by about 20 percentage points.
goal_06.content.#11.scenes.#1.text: Across all regions with available data, rural access to safely managed drinking water has increased, but still lags behind urban access.
goal_06.content.#11.scenes.#2.text: South Asia is an exception: the urban–rural gap narrowed sharply between 2000 and 2024 as access expanded in rural areas. Sub-Saharan Africa has also seen gains in rural access, but coverage remains very low—only 17 percent of the rural population had safely managed drinking water in 2024.
goal_06.content.#12.text: Next, we examine the overall levels of access to safely managed drinking water access at the country level. While data availability has improved in recent years, approximately one third of countries still lack water quality data, due to insufficient water monitoring or testing systems.
goal_06.content.#13.title: Low safely managed drinking water access persists in Sub-Saharan Africa and parts of the Pacific
goal_06.content.#13.subtitle: People using safely managed drinking water services, 2024 (% of population)
goal_06.content.#13.source: WHO/UNICEF JMP for Water Supply, Sanitation and Hygiene. Retrieved from World Development Indicators (SH.H2O.SMDW.ZS).
goal_06.content.#13.note: The 69 countries shown in gray diagonal hatching have no available estimates of safely managed drinking water access in 2024.
goal_06.content.#13.scenes.#0.text: In 2024, data on access to safely managed drinking water services are available for 148 countries.
goal_06.content.#13.scenes.#1.text: Twenty-nine countries have achieved universal access (99 percent or higher) to safely managed drinking water.
goal_06.content.#13.scenes.#2.text: Low access is concentrated in Sub-Saharan Africa, where fewer than half of the population has access (in 23 of 26 countries with data). Central African Republic and Chad are among the lowest: only approximately 6 percent of the population have access to safe drinking water. For both countries accessibility is the limiting factor, but water quality and availability also face great challenges.
goal_06.content.#14.text: How is access to safely managed drinking water estimated?
goal_06.content.#15.text: For a household to qualify as having safely managed drinking water it needs access to an [emphasis: improved water source] that is: (1) [emphasis: on premises (easily accessible)], (2) [emphasis: available when needed (readily available)], and (3) [emphasis: free from contamination (of good quality)]. Because the data for these three components often come from different sources and surveys, they cannot always be measured for the same population. To avoid overstating access, the methodology uses a conservative approach: the lowest value among the three components listed above is taken as the estimate of safely managed drinking water access. The component with the lowest value is also referred to as the limiting factor.
goal_06.content.#16.title: How is safely managed drinking water access estimated?
goal_06.content.#16.subtitle: People using improved water source (%), 2024
goal_06.content.#16.source: WHO/UNICEF JMP for Water Supply, Sanitation and Hygiene
goal_06.content.#16.scenes.#0.text: For example, [emphasis: in Mongolia], 84 percent of individuals with improved water sources have available water when needed, and 82 percent access uncontaminated water. However, [emphasis: only 43 percent have improved water sources at home], resulting in an estimate of 43 percent of people with safely managed drinking water access.
goal_06.content.#16.scenes.#1.text: Conversely, [emphasis: in Nepal], accessibility and availability are relatively high (79 percent and 82 percent, respectively), but [emphasis: only 16 percent have access to uncontaminated water], leading to a safely managed drinking water access estimate of merely 16 percent. So, in the case of Nepal, “free from contamination” is the limiting factor.
goal_06.content.#17.text: Water quality is the biggest challenge
goal_06.content.#18.text: Across 94 countries with available data[footnote:  Excludes 29 countries with universal access (99%+) and 25 countries lacking availability data (54 in total). Safely managed drinking water access is estimated when all three components are available, or when water quality data are available with at least one other component.], [emphasis: water quality is most commonly the component with the lowest value]. Water quality is the limiting factor in 48 countries (51 percent), followed by accessibility in 23 countries (24.5 percent) and availability in 23 countries (24.5 percent).
goal_06.content.#19.title: Countries face different challenges in achieving safely managed drinking water access
goal_06.content.#19.subtitle: People using improved water source (%), 2024
goal_06.content.#19.source: WHO/UNICEF JMP for Water Supply, Sanitation and Hygiene
goal_06.content.#19.keyFactLabel: of countries (48 out of 94) with data face water quality challenges—the most common constraint among the three components.
goal_06.content.#19.scenes.#0.text: In several Pacific Island nations, including Tuvalu, Nauru, and Tonga, [emphasis: water quality] is extremely low compared to availability and accessibility. Many small atoll islands have no rivers or large lakes. Most drinking water comes from rainwater harvesting, and systems are easily contaminated or diluted by saltwater, while water treatment capacity is limited.[reference: waterq1][reference: waterq2] Other countries which face significant water quality challenges include a few Southeast Asian countries—such as Cambodia, Indonesia, and Lao PDR.
goal_06.content.#19.scenes.#1.text: [emphasis: Accessibility] is a major challenge in many Sub-Saharan African countries, such as Democratic Republic of the Congo, Malawi, Guinea-Bissau, Lesotho and Sao Tome and Principe, where access to water on premises remains limited. These countries often face infrastructure shortages,[reference: wateracc1] particularly in rural areas where communities are widely dispersed and infrastructure investments tend to prioritize urban populations.[reference: wateracc2]
goal_06.content.#19.scenes.#2.text: In countries with water scarcity,[reference: waterav1][reference: waterav2] seasonal fluctuations in water availability,[reference: waterav3] and infrastructure challenges, [reference: waterav4] [emphasis: availability] is the lowest component. Bhutan, South Africa, Algeria, and Trinidad and Tobago are among the countries with the lowest levels of availability.
goal_06.content.#19.scenes.#3.text: The map presents the limiting factors for safely managed drinking water access in 94 countries.Please note that gray diagonal hatching indicates countries that have attained universal access, countries where safely managed drinking water data exist but availability component data are missing, or countries where safely managed drinking water data are not available.
goal_06.content.#21.text: Among the three components of safely managed drinking water — accessibility, availability, and quality — water quality is not only the most common constraint across countries, but the progress in improving it has also lagged behind the other two. While many countries have expanded physical access to water and improved availability, ensuring that water is free from contamination remains more challenging. Tanzania illustrates this pattern clearly.
goal_06.content.#22.title: Progress speed varies across service components in Tanzania
goal_06.content.#22.subtitle: People using improved water sources by service component (%), 2024; progress speed
goal_06.content.#22.source: WHO/UNICEF JMP for Water Supply, Sanitation and Hygiene; World Bank staff calculations.
goal_06.content.#22.note: For details on the progress speed, refer to the Explainer and Explore Data sections.
goal_06.content.#22.scenes.#0.text: In 2000, just 1 percent of Tanzanians had water on premises. At the same time, nearly 15 percent had access to uncontaminated water.
goal_06.content.#22.scenes.#1.text: Over the next two decades, infrastructure expanded rapidly: by 2024, almost half of the population had water on premises. Yet only about one-third had access to uncontaminated water. Accessibility improved dramatically; water quality lagged behind.
goal_06.content.#22.scenes.#2.text: Is this pattern unique to Tanzania? To answer that question, we look at the average path of progress across countries from 2000 to 2024. Using historical country-level data, we estimate a “typical” trajectory of improvement for each component of safely managed drinking water (see methodology on this page [progress-paper]).
goal_06.content.#22.scenes.#3.text: The contrast is striking. Increasing water accessibility — bringing coverage from 40 percent of the population to 75 percent — would take less than 36 years along the typical path.
goal_06.content.#22.scenes.#4.text: Achieving the same scale of progress in water quality would take nearly three times longer — more than a century.
goal_06.content.#24.text: Improving water quality is often more challenging than improving the other two components—accessibility and availability—because the latter can often be achieved through one-time infrastructure investments. By contrast, maintaining water quality requires continuous management of pollution sources and sustained monitoring, making progress slower and more complex.[reference: who_guideline][reference: wb_water_quality][reference: unesco_water_quality] In addition, measuring water quality presents its own challenges: household surveys require additional resources such as testing equipment and trained staff, whereas information on water source and availability can be collected more easily through standard questionnaires. [reference: water_test]
goal_06.content.#26.text: Water quality can also be compromised by human activities ranging from household practices to large-scale agriculture and industry. Sewage and other wastes are often discharged into rivers, lakes, and wetlands, allowing fecal contamination to spread through interconnected water systems—especially when wastewater is untreated. Untreated human excreta is one of the most significant sources of contamination, closely linked to the lack of safely managed sanitation.[reference: link_san][reference: link_bacteria]
goal_06.content.#28.text: The presence of the bacteria, Escherichia coli (E. coli), is the key indicator of fecal contamination of drinking water.
goal_06.content.#29.text: Water contaminated by human feces can lead to various acute diseases and sometimes pose life-threatening risks, particularly to infants and young children who are most vulnerable as their immune systems are still developing. Every year, half a million people die from diarrheal diseases due to drinking unsafe water, more than half of whom (54 percent) are children younger than five years. [reference: burden_disease]
goal_06.content.#30.text: To assess fecal contamination through household surveys, the presence of E. coli is measured in a 100 mL water sample. According to WHO guidelines, water is classified as low risk if it contains fewer than 1 E. coli per 100 mL, moderate risk if it contains 1–10, high risk if it contains 11–100, and very high risk if it exceeds 100 E. coli per 100 mL.
goal_06.content.#31.title: Risk levels of fecal contamination
goal_06.content.#31.subtitle: E.coli CFU/100mL
goal_06.content.#31.source: WHO, UNICEF, World Bank. State of the world’s drinking water. Geneva: World Health Organization; 2022. Licence: CC BY-NC-SA 3.0 IGO.
goal_06.content.#31.note: CFU (colony-forming units) measures the number of viable E. coli bacteria in a water sample, expressed as CFU per 100 mL.
goal_06.content.#32.text: In household surveys such as the Multiple Indicator Cluster Surveys (MICS), water quality testing is conducted both at the point of collection and at the point of use[emphasis: ]within the household. [emphasis: Point of collection ]refers to[emphasis: a water sample taken at the household’s primary drinking water source] (e.g., tap, well, protected spring) and tested for E. coli to assess contamination at the source before transport or storage. [emphasis: Point of use ]refers to[emphasis: a water sample taken within the household ]— typically from a drinking glass or container — and tested for E. coli to assess contamination present at the point of consumption after transport, storage, and handling.
goal_06.content.#33.text: This approach makes it possible to assess contamination at the source and to identify additional contamination that may occur during transport or storage, for example through unhygienic or uncovered containers, dirty ladles or cups, or contaminated hands.[reference: ecoli_storage]
goal_06.content.#34.title: E. coli contamination at the point of use is higher than at the point of collection
goal_06.content.#34.subtitle: 30 countries with the largest shares of the population using drinking water sources with high or very high faecal contamination risk (%)
goal_06.content.#34.keyFactLabel: have more than half of their population exposed to high or very high E. coli contamination risk in drinking water.
goal_06.content.#34.scenes.#0.text: Let’s look at the shares of people who use water with high and very high E. coli risk levels at[emphasis: point of collection]. In 15 countries, at least half of the population collects drinking water which already has high or very high levels of E. coli contamination.
goal_06.content.#34.scenes.#1.text: The contamination rates are even higher for the share of people whose water has high and very high E. coli risk levels at [emphasis: point of use]. In 26 out of 30 countries with data, more than half of the people drink water with high or very high risk of E.coli contamination, as shown by tests conducted in the household.
goal_06.content.#34.scenes.#2.text: In 28 out of the 30 countries, contamination levels [emphasis: at the point of use] were higher than at [emphasis: point of collection]. For example, in Malawi, the share of the population exposed to high or very high levels of contamination was 36 percent at point of collection, but rose 81 percent at point of use.
goal_06.content.#35.text: In many countries with available data, households store water prior to consumption, which can increase the risk of contamination.
goal_06.content.#36.text: As a result, household water treatment — such as boiling, chlorination, filtration, or solar disinfection — is an important public health intervention to reduce bacterial and other microbial contamination. However, treatment practices are often uncommon, and when reported, they may not be effective due to incorrect or inconsistent application. [reference: ecoli_research]
goal_06.content.#37.text: E.coli contamination: urban-rural gap
goal_06.content.#38.text: Urban–rural disparities in water contamination are widespread. In most countries with available survey data, rural populations face substantially higher risks from contaminated drinking water than their urban counterparts.
goal_06.content.#40.text: For example, [emphasis: in Fiji, 56 percent of rural residents use water sources classified as high or very high risk,] compared to 4 percent of urban residents. Many rural communities in Fiji are geographically isolated and depend on untreated sources such as surface water, rainwater, wells, and boreholes. Climate change and increasingly severe extreme weather events intensify the risk of microbial contamination.[reference: fiji1] Similarly[emphasis: in Togo and in the Republic of the Congo, the share of rural residents using high-risk water sources is far higher than that of urban residents]—72 percent versus 26 percent, and 56 percent versus 10 percent, respectively.
goal_06.content.#41.title: Exposure to E. coli contamination is higher among rural residents than urban residents
goal_06.content.#41.subtitle: The 10 countries with the largest urban–rural gap in exposure to high or very high contamination risk at the point of collection (%)
goal_06.content.#41.source: Multiple Indicator Cluster Surveys (MICS);[reference: MICS_survey] Ethiopia: 2016 Ethiopia Socioeconomic Survey (ESS).[reference: Ethiopia_survey]
goal_06.content.#41.note: The data are compiled from country-level survey reports, including MICS and the Ethiopian Socioeconomic Survey ESS.
goal_06.content.#43.text: Beyond risks from fecal contamination, [emphasis: chemical contaminants such as arsenic, fluoride, nitrates, and heavy metals also pose serious long-term health threats]. Under SDG target 6.1, arsenic and fluoride are priority concerns. At low concentrations, fluoride strengthens teeth; however, excessive exposure can lead to adverse effects on skeletal tissues, including dental and skeletal fluorosis,[reference: who_guideline] characterized by changes in bone and tooth structure. Arsenic contamination is likewise harmful, with prolonged exposure[reference: arsenic_exposure] associated with skin lesions, cancers, cardiovascular disease, and cognitive impairment.
goal_06.content.#44.text: Drinking water contaminated with arsenic in Bangladesh
goal_06.content.#45.text: Arsenic is a naturally occurring element found in the earth’s crust. About 140 million people[reference: arsenic_exposure] in at least 70 countries consume water containing arsenic at levels above WHO’s guideline value of 10 parts per billion (ppb).[reference: who_guideline] [emphasis: The largest numbers of people exposed to arsenic in drinking water are in][emphasis: Bangladesh and India][reference: arsenic_bgd]. Bangladesh faces the world’s most severe arsenic crisis: since the 1990s, millions have been exposed, leading to cancers, skin disease, and adverse pregnancy outcomes.
goal_06.content.#46.text: In Bangladesh, around 17 percent of the population drinks water with arsenic concentrations above the WHO guideline. Rural residents face higher risks than urban residents, and poorer households are more likely to be exposed than wealthier ones.
goal_06.content.#47.title: Rural dwellers and poorer people  are more exposed to arsenic-contaminated water.
goal_06.content.#47.subtitle: Proportion of the population by arsenic contamination level, residence, and wealth quintile (%)
goal_06.content.#47.source: Bangladesh MICS 2019: Water Quality Thematic Report
goal_06.content.#47.scenes.#0.text: Rural dwellers are more exposed to arsenic-contaminated water than urban dwellers. While 12 percent of the people in rural areas are exposed to arsenic levels exceeding 50 ppb, only 5 percent of the people in urban areas face the same challenge.
goal_06.content.#47.scenes.#1.text: When we look at the arsenic contamination by wealth quintiles, people in the poorer and middle wealth quintiles face greater exposure than the wealthiest quintile.
goal_06.content.#48.text: There are also regional differences in arsenic contamination in drinking water. When we look at the data in division level, Sylhet has the highest proportion of population with arsenic contaminated water above WHO level of 10 ppb (35 percent), followed by Chattogram (29 percent) and Khulna (20 percent).  In contrast, exposure is very limited in Barishal, where only 1 percent of the population exceeds the WHO guideline.
goal_06.content.#49.title: Regional differences in arsenic levels
goal_06.content.#49.subtitle: Proportion of the population by district, Bangladesh (% of district population)
goal_06.content.#49.source: Bangladesh MICS 2019: Water Quality Thematic Report
goal_06.content.#49.legendLabel: Arsenic concentration in groundwater above 10 ppb (%)
goal_06.content.#50.text: [emphasis: Arsenic exposure is particularly high among users of wells, including tubewells, which are the most common source of drinking water in Bangladesh. ]Tubewells are a relatively low cost way to access shallow groundwater, which is generally less prone to bacterial contamination than surface water. However, in Bangladesh, due to geological conditions, many of these sources of water, located just a few meters below the ground, are contaminated with arsenic.[reference: arsenic_bgd].
goal_06.content.#51.text: Among people who rely on wells, roughly one in five (about 20 percent) are exposed to arsenic concentrations above the WHO guideline of 10 ppb. This risk is substantially lower for users of other water sources: only about 5 percent of those with piped water and 1.5 percent of those using alternative sources exceed this level. At higher concentrations, above the national standard of 50 ppb, the same pattern holds. About 12 percent of well users are exposed at these levels, compared with 3 percent of piped-water users and just 0.5 percent of those relying on other sources.
goal_06.content.#52.title: Arsenic contamination levels are highest in wells - including tubewells - in Bangladesh.
goal_06.content.#52.subtitle: Proportion of household population by arsenic contamination levels and by water sources (%)
goal_06.content.#52.source: Bangladesh MICS 2019: Water Quality Thematic Report
goal_06.content.#52.note: Tubewells are narrow, drilled groundwater wells fitted with a pump to draw water from underground aquifers.
goal_06.content.#53.text: The government’s initiatives and programs to solve arsenic issues
goal_06.content.#54.text: Since the 1990s, the Government of Bangladesh has introduced a wide range of programs to address arsenic contamination in drinking water. These efforts have included nationwide screening of tubewells, the installation of arsenic-safe water sources such as piped systems drawing on safe surface or deep groundwater, and the use of household and community filtration technologies. Public awareness campaigns have also been carried out, alongside health sector initiatives to identify and manage arsenic-related illnesses.
goal_06.content.#55.text: Despite these initiatives, arsenic contamination remains a pressing challenge. Millions of people continue to rely on unsafe water because arsenic-free options—such as deep wells, piped systems, or rainwater harvesting—are often unavailable, unaffordable, or too distant[reference: arsenic_bgd]. Research[reference: arsenic_research] indicates that limited resources, gaps in local information, and the sheer number of people and wells affected further complicate mitigation efforts. Countries like Bangladesh face the dual burden of microbial risks and arsenic contamination, a problem that is particularly difficult for low- and middle-income countries with constrained resources.
goal_06.content.#56.text: Why Water Quality Matters: Challenges and the Way Forward
goal_06.content.#57.text: Safely managed drinking water is fundamental to human health and development, yet major challenges remain in ensuring water quality for all. While global access has progressed over the past two decades, deep inequalities persist across regions, countries, income groups, and between rural and urban areas. A major challenge is that in many low- and middle-income countries, drinking water is still contaminated by E. coli or harmful chemicals such as arsenic.
goal_06.content.#58.text: At the same time, strengthening sanitation and hygiene systems is critical, but sanitation programs, in particular, remain chronically underfunded[reference: SWS] in many countries.  Without adequate sanitation and hand hygiene, investments in water supply are undermined by contamination, perpetuating cycles of disease.
goal_06.content.#59.text: As Nobel laureate Michael Kremer emphasizes, the link between water quality, access to safe drinking water, and child survival is especially strong in low- and middle-income countries. Evidence shows[reference: Kremer] that improved water treatment can reduce child mortality by 25 percent, with the greatest benefits for infants and young children.
goal_06.references.chemical_risks: Rodrigues, E.G., Bellinger, D.C., Valeri, L. et al. Neurodevelopmental outcomes among 2- to 3-year-old children in Bangladesh with elevated blood lead and exposure to arsenic and manganese in drinking water. Environ Health 15, 44 (2016).

goal_06.references.waterav1: FAO. Water efficiency, productivity and sustainability in the NENA regions (WEPS-NENA)
